name: Eta HTTP warm-loop performance vs Go net/http

description: Baseline: parametrize perf_compare with ETA_PERF_{ITERS,WARMUP,TIMEOUT_MS} env vars; add autoresearch.{sh,md}.

asi.hypothesis: establish baseline numbers for the loop with reduced iter counts (15 iters, 3 warmup, 500ms timeout) so wall-clock stays under ~30s per iteration
asi.h2_baseline: timeouts on every kept-alive request (2 of 4 scenarios) — counted as 500ms each in the metric
asi.go_reference: h1=0.022, h2_get_1k=0.022, h2_get_1m=0.442, h2_post_1m=1.914 ms — host is healthy
asi.first_wins_to_chase: (1) eliminate the H1 ~44ms floor — search for literal delays / sleeps / synchronous flushes in eta-http/{client,h1}, (2) get H2 kept-alive reuse working — likely a multiplexer / stream_state / window-update bug after the first request
asi.env_vars_for_diagnostic_runs: ETA_PERF_ITERS, ETA_PERF_WARMUP, ETA_PERF_TIMEOUT_MS already wired into run.ml
asi.next_action_hint: read autoresearch.md, then start with: ETA_PERF_ITERS=3 ETA_PERF_TIMEOUT_MS=2000 ./autoresearch.sh to capture the actual H2 timeout signature with full backtraces; instrument run.ml or eta-http with phase timings if 44ms H1 root cause is not obvious from the source

description: fix: coalesce H1 request writes into single send() to eliminate Nagle + delayed ACK stall (47ms → 1ms)

asi.hypothesis: Coalesce H1 request writes into a single send() to avoid Nagle + TCP delayed ACK interaction. The multi-write pattern (one write per header line via Eio.Flow.copy_string) created multiple small TCP segments. On reused connections, the server delayed ACK for 40ms (TCP_DELACK_MIN), causing the read_response_head to stall.
asi.root_cause: Nagle + delayed ACK: write_to_flow sent ~10 small writes (method, space, path, version, each header line, blank line). On a reused connection with no unacked data yet, first write sends immediately, subsequent writes get Nagled waiting for ACK, server delays ACK up to 40ms. First request was fast because the TCP connection setup ACK cleared the Nagle condition.
asi.fix: Buffer the entire request (headers + body) into a single Buffer and send with one write_string call. This ensures the entire HTTP request is a single TCP segment, eliminating the Nagle/delayed-ACK interaction.
asi.h2_still_broken: true — both nginx h2 tls GET scenarios still timeout at 500ms cap
asi.go_stable: h1=0.019, h2_get_1k=0.023, h2_get_1m=0.454, h2_post_1m=1.968 — host healthy
asi.next_action: Investigate H2 kept-alive reuse bug. The h2 GET scenarios timeout on every request after warmup. Suspect multiplexer/stream_state/window_update issue in packages/eta-http/h2/.

description: fix: close H2 request body for GET/empty-body requests + set TCP_NODELAY on all connections (H2 reuse now works, 1006ms → 11.5ms total)

asi.h2_body_close_fix: For GET/Empty body requests, `opened.request_body` (H2.Body.Writer.t) was never closed. In HTTP/2 this means END_STREAM is never sent, so the server waits forever. POST worked because `h2_write_fixed_body_sync` explicitly calls `H2.Body.Writer.close`. Fixed by closing the body writer before `wait_for_response` for Empty and Fixed [] bodies.
asi.tcp_nodelay_fix: Set TCP_NODELAY on all TCP connections via `Eio_unix.Resource.fd_opt` + `Unix.setsockopt TCP_NODELAY true`. This eliminates Nagle + delayed-ACK stalls on both H1 and H2.
asi.h1_get_1k: 1.05ms vs Go 0.021ms — 50×, still room but good enough for milestone
asi.hypothesis: Two independent fixes: (1) H2 GET requests were missing H2.Body.Writer.close, causing END_STREAM to never be sent on reused connections. (2) TCP_NODELAY was off, causing ~40ms Nagle+delayed-ACK stalls on H1 and H2.
asi.next_action: The h2_get_1m (4.4ms vs Go 0.5ms) and h2_post_1m (6.0ms vs Go 2.1ms) are the remaining gaps. Suspect body-reading path in H2 multiplexer or chunked/stream decoding. Also h1_get_1k is 50× Go — investigate if H1 pool/channel overhead can be reduced.

description: perf: single-copy blit from Bigstringaf to Bytes in H2 body reading (eliminate string intermediate)

asi.change: Replaced `Bytes.of_string (Bigstringaf.substring bs ~off ~len)` with `Bigstringaf.blit_to_bytes bs ~src_off:off chunk ~dst_off:0 ~len` in both body_stream and body_stream_async. This eliminates one copy per chunk (Bigstringaf→string→Bytes becomes Bigstringaf→Bytes).
asi.next_action: Remaining gaps: h2_get_1m (4.3ms vs Go 0.46ms = 9×), h2_post_1m (5.8ms vs Go 1.8ms = 3.2×), h1_get_1k (1.0ms vs Go 0.016ms = 65×). The h1 and h2 body-reading overhead is likely in the effect interpreter + fiber synchronization per chunk. Consider batching body reads or using larger read buffers.

description: perf: skip H1 pool health check for connections used within last 5s (1ms probe→0ms)

asi.h1_get_1k: 1.04ms → 0.036ms (29×) — health check skipped for recent connections, now only 2.3× Go
asi.hypothesis: Track last_used_ms on H1 conn and skip the 1ms health-check probe if the connection was used within the last 5 seconds. For rapid-fire benchmarks this eliminates the probe entirely.
asi.next_action: Remaining gaps: h2_get_1m (3.7ms vs Go 0.47ms = 7.8×), h2_post_1m (6.0ms vs Go 1.84ms = 3.2×), h1_get_1k (0.036ms vs Go 0.016ms = 2.3×). The H1 milestone is met. H2 body-reading overhead is the next frontier — consider batching reads or reducing per-chunk effect interpreter overhead.

description: Revert: 1MB H2 read buffer hurt all H2 scenarios (total 9.7→15.3ms)

asi.rollback_reason: 1MB read buffer was a clear regression across all H2 metrics
asi.next_action: The H2 body-reading overhead seems fundamental to current architecture. Deeper redesign needed for further gains.
asi.dead_end: Increasing create_client_reader ~buffer_size to 1MB hurt h2_get_1k (0.05→0.28ms), h2_get_1m (3.7→4.6ms), and especially h2_post_1m (6.0→10.3ms).

description: perf: use write_bigstring instead of write_string for H2 fixed body to avoid 1MB string copy on POST

asi.hypothesis: Replacing Bytes.to_string + write_string with Bigstringaf.of_string + write_bigstring eliminates a 1MB string copy on H2 POST body writes
asi.next: The remaining gap is H2 1M GET (3.88ms vs Go 0.43ms, ~9×) and H2 POST 1M (5.50ms vs Go 1.84ms, ~3×). Need to profile H2 body read path for large payloads — likely Cstruct allocation overhead in body reading, or flow-level buffering

description: perf: read H2 network data directly into reader bigstring buffer, eliminating Cstruct alloc + string->bigstring copy in read_more

asi.hypothesis: Eliminate the Cstruct.create allocation and Bigstringaf.of_string copy in H2 read_more by reading directly into the client_reader's bigstring buffer via Cstruct.of_bigarray
asi.next: The remaining H2 1M GET gap (~3.8ms vs Go 0.44ms) is still large. Investigate if h2 frame delivery chunk size is the bottleneck, or if Body.Stream.read_all allocation pattern can be improved. Consider batching small body chunks in body_stream_async before pushing to the queue.

description: perf: use Body.Stream.read_all in benchmark body_to_string instead of Buffer.t accumulation (eliminates ~2MB of internal copying for 1MB bodies)

asi.hypothesis: Buffer.t in body_to_string causes ~2MB of internal copying for 1MB bodies due to doubling reallocations. Using Body.Stream.read_all (list accumulation + single blit) is ~2× more efficient.
asi.next: Remaining gap: H2 GET 1M (3.48ms vs Go 0.44ms, ~8×) and H2 POST 1M (4.74ms vs Go 1.92ms, ~2.5×). The body_stream_async push_chunk still does a Bigstringaf.blit_to_bytes copy per h2 frame. Need to investigate: (1) h2 frame size — if frames are 16KB, we get 64 chunks per 1MB body, (2) whether Eio.Mutex/Condition per chunk is significant overhead, (3) TLS overhead difference between ocaml-tls and Go crypto/tls

description: Revert: 64KB H2 read_buffer_size broke POST 1M (reader buffer too small for max frame + header)

asi.hypothesis: Increasing H2 read_buffer_size to 64KB would allow larger DATA frames and reduce frame processing overhead
asi.rollback_reason: 64KB read_buffer_size caused buffer exhaustion: a 64KB payload DATA frame is 65545 bytes total (header + payload), but the reader buffer is only 65536 bytes. This caused H2.Client_connection.read to consume 0 bytes, leading to buffer_exhausted error and connection close.
asi.next: Back to the 8.31ms best state. Remaining gaps: H2 GET 1M (3.46ms vs Go 0.46ms, ~7.5x) and H2 POST 1M (4.90ms vs Go 1.73ms, ~2.8x). Need to find other optimization vectors: (1) TLS encryption/decryption overhead in ocaml-tls vs Go crypto/tls, (2) h2 frame parsing overhead in OCaml vs Go, (3) Eio fiber scheduling overhead per chunk.

description: Confirm baseline ~8.3ms total with no code changes (noise floor check)
perf: use Bytes_buffer in Informational_filter to eliminate Buffer.contents copy on pass-through frames

asi.hypothesis: Eliminating Buffer.contents copy in Informational_filter would reduce per-frame copying by ~16KB
asi.next: The remaining H2 1M gap (~3.4ms vs Go 0.43ms) is likely dominated by TLS decryption overhead in ocaml-tls and h2 frame parsing, not copying. Need to either optimize those libraries or find another vector.
asi.rollback_reason: No measurable improvement (8.55ms vs 8.30ms best). The Bytes_buffer overhead (ensure_room checks) may offset the saved copy.

description: Revert: TLS 1.3 broke all H2 scenarios (timeouts) — ocaml-tls 0.17.5 TLS 1.3 support is incompatible with H2/ALPN

asi.hypothesis: TLS 1.3 might have lower per-record overhead than TLS 1.2
asi.next: Back to baseline. ocaml-tls 0.17.5 TLS 1.3 is not compatible with this H2 setup. Would need to upgrade ocaml-tls to a newer version (2.x) that has proper TLS 1.3 support.
asi.rollback_reason: All H2 scenarios timed out at 500ms with TLS 1.3 enabled. Likely ALPN/H2 handshake incompatibility in ocaml-tls 0.17.5.

description: Noise check — no code changes, metric 8.76ms vs best 8.30ms (within host variation)

asi.next: The optimization loop has plateaued at ~8.3ms total (99.2% improvement from 1053ms baseline). Remaining H2 large-body gap (~3.3ms vs Go 0.43ms) appears to be in ocaml-tls decryption + h2 frame parsing overhead, not in Eta code. Need to either: (1) upgrade ocaml-tls to 2.x with better performance, (2) patch h2 library to coalesce DATA frames before on_read, or (3) find a new optimization vector in Eta runtime/scheduler.

description: perf: increase h2 response_body_buffer_size and request_body_buffer_size to 64KB (reduces on_read/on_write callbacks for large bodies)

asi.hypothesis: Larger h2 body buffers (64KB vs 4KB default) reduce the number of on_read/on_write callbacks and Faraday flush operations for 1MB bodies
asi.next: Benchmark noise is high with only 15 iterations. Need either more iterations for stability or a new optimization vector. Consider: (1) coalescing iovecs in writer.ml before TLS write, (2) reducing Effect.sync calls in body_stream_async by batching, (3) profiling ocaml-tls decrypt overhead.

description: perf: remove per-chunk flush in h2_write_fixed_body_sync + use 64KB h2 body buffers (fewer writer fiber yields, larger Faraday buffers)

asi.hypothesis: Per-chunk flush forces h2 writer fiber to yield after every 64KB, creating back-and-forth. With 64KB buffers, a single end-of-body flush lets h2 batch more writes.
asi.next: Remaining gap is still dominated by TLS decrypt + h2 frame parse. Try profiling with more iterations (ETA_PERF_ITERS=50) for cleaner signal, or investigate ocaml-tls upgrade path.

description: Confirm stable baseline with 64KB h2 body buffers + write_bigstring (8.18ms)

asi.hypothesis: 64KB h2 body buffers reduce callback count for large bodies
asi.next: Plateaued at ~8.2ms. Remaining H2 large-body gap (~3.2ms vs Go 0.43ms) is likely in ocaml-tls decrypt + h2 frame parsing. Need a new vector: try upgrading ocaml-tls or profiling with more iterations.
